question: Case: B A 10-year-old boy was brought to casualty with fever and breathlessness. Presently the child was complaining of aches and pains, and tiredness. On examination, he was anaemic, icteric and showed pallor and signs of retarded growth and development. An increased reticulocyte count was also reported in this smear. Electrophoresis of haemoglobin was performed to detect haemoglobin structural variant, if any. It revealed presence of HbS. Based on these tests, the child was diagnosed as having sickle cell anaemia. (a) What is the molecular defect in sickle cell anaemia? (b) What is sickling crisis? question:
A 10-year-old boy was brought to casualty with fever and breathlessness. Earlier he had upper respiratory tract infection. Presently the child was complaining of aches and pains, and tiredness. On examination, he was anaemic, icteric and showed pallor and signs of retarded growth and development. His blood pressure was 98/68 mmHg and pulse was 100/ minute, with wide pulse pressure. The sclera was yellow, abdomen was distended and the spleen was enlarged. The child was admitted for the treatment of respiratory tract infection and for further investigations. 
Results of some of the blood tests are as here. 

Test – Patient’s reports – Reference range 
• Haemoglobin – 5.2 g/dL ↓ – 13–16 g/dL 
• Red Blood cells – 2 × 10¹²/L ↓ – 4.5–6.5 × 10¹²/L 
• Platelets – 1,90,000/mL – 1,50,000–4,00,000/mL 
• Total Bilirubin – 4.8 mg/dL ↑ – 0.1–1.0 mg/dL 
• Direct Bilirubin – 0.6 mg/dL – Up to 0.4 mg/dL 
• Alanine transaminase – 48 U/L – 10–45 U/L 
• Alkaline phosphatase – 124 U/L – 40–100 U/L 
• Lactate dehydrogenase – 605 U/L ↑ – 100–300 U/L 
An increased reticulocyte count was also reported in this smear. 
Electrophoresis of haemoglobin was performed to detect haemoglobin structural variant, if any. It revealed presence of HbS. Based on these tests; the child was diagnosed as having sickle cell anaemia. 

a) What is the molecular defect in sickle cell anemia? Which are other hemoglobinopathies? 
b) What is sickling crisis? 
c) What is the cause of increased bilirubin in this patient? 
d) Why Lactate dehydrogenase level is very high? 
e) What is the normal life span of RBC? Why it is reduced in sickle cell anemia? Why these patients are protected against malaria?

Why hypercholesterolemia predisposes to cardiac disease?

question: A 3 years old tribal boy was brought in paediatric OPD with severe pain in abdomen. Detailed history revealed that he was born normal but over the period of time, his development was delayed and had similar type of acute abdominal attacks earlier too. On examination: Severe pallor of skin, mild icterus & hepatosplenomegaly was present. Lab investigation showed: Hb: 6.2 g/dl, Total bilirubin: 4.2 mg/dl, indirect bilirubin: 3.2 mg/dl, Sickle solubility test showed less soluble Hb. The pediatrician made provisional diagnosis of Sickle cell anaemia. 1. Name two confirmatory tests to diagnose Sickle cell disease. 2. Write biochemical justification for anemia & jaundice in this patient. 3. Why high altitude can precipitate such attacks? 4. Why sickle cell patients are resistant to malaria? 5. Name the type of genetic mutation involved in sickle cell disease.

question: A 62-year-old patient was diagnosed as having decompensated liver cirrhosis due to hepatitis C virus one year earlier. He is currently admitted to the hospital because of generalized tonic-clonic seizures for several hours and loss of consciousness. Laboratory data showed anemia, thrombopenia, increased transaminase, alkaline phosphatase and total bilirubin, and decreased albumin, total cholesterol and BTR (branched chain amino acid/tyrosine ratio). Furthermore, the plasma ammonia (NH3) level on admission was twice the normal level and Urea is less than 10 mg/dl. i. What is the likely diagnosis? ii. Why is the ammonia level high and urea level low in this patient? iii. Why is high ammonia level toxic to the Brain? iv. Name the key enzyme of Urea cycle? v. How is lactulose useful in the management of these patients?

question: A 40 year old man is brought to the emergency in a semi-conscious state. His blood glucose was found to be low & a dextrose drip was started. History was sought from the family, who explained that he is a chronic alcoholic, with recurrent episodes of jaundice in past. On examination he has ascitis, pedal oedema & jaundice. His Laboratory report is: Random Blood Sugar: 55 mg/dl Total Bilirubin: 25.3 mg/dl Direct Bilirubin: 18.7 mg/dl The provisional diagnosis is Chronic alcoholic hepatitis. USG abdomen revealed fatty changes in the liver, hepatomegaly & a solitary nodule in left lobe with ill-defined margins. i. What are the metabolic effects of alcohol metabolism? ii. How chronic alcoholism predisposes to fatty liver? iii. Name two lipotropic factors. iv. Which tumour marker may be used to screen the patient for Hepatocellular carcinoma? v. What may be the cause for hypoglycemia in this patient?
